html:
HSS Engineers Bhd, MYX:HSSEB

At RM0.455, HSS Engineers is a RM231 million Malaysian engineering and project-management micro-cap being valued as a weak cash-conversion consultancy, not as the owner of one of the largest visible infrastructure consulting backlogs on Bursa. That skepticism is partly earned. FY2025 revenue rose 11% to RM222 million, but PATAMI fell to RM13.7 million, Q4 profit nearly vanished, operating cash flow was negative, and borrowings rose to RM78 million against RM26 million of cash and deposits. The old “net cash floor” thesis is wrong.

The better thesis is more nuanced: HSS is a bruised, working-capital-stretched, asset-light infrastructure consultant with roughly RM2.15 billion to RM2.3 billion of unbilled order book, nearly 10x annual revenue, and several live paths to earnings normalization. At today’s price, the stock is 16.9x depressed trailing earnings, but only about 12x Mercury’s FY2026 RM19 million estimate and 10.5x FY2027 RM22 million estimate. If provisions fade and even modest backlog conversion lifts profit back toward RM25 million to RM30 million, the stock is 8x to 9x earnings. If MRT3 and Baghdad both move from paperwork and recognition disputes into funded execution, the multiple collapses into the mid-single digits.

The central variant perception is that the market is treating the order book as trapped accounting value. That is too harsh. The RM997.9 million MRT3 Circle Line PMC contract runs for 11 years, and the project received final railway scheme approval in July 2025, with land acquisition targeted by end-2026. This is not yet a tunneling-and-piling revenue boom, so the prior bullish framing was too early. But the contract is real, the political approval milestone is real, and the market now gives HSS little credit for a 2027 acceleration.

Baghdad is the messier but higher-torque piece. The JV contract is US$315.9 million for PMC and supervision services through May 2029. Phase 1 has reportedly reached 50% completion, while HSS recognized 37% prudently, but cash collection has lagged because billing depends on funds from Baghdad authorities. This created contract assets and financing stress. The next major rerating trigger is not a glossy project update, it is cash receipts. The reported first US$1.5 million tranche and second US$3 million invoice matter because they would prove collectability and reduce the “fake revenue” discount.

The domestic book is more dependable. Pan Borneo Sabah Phase 1A was 86.6% complete in early 2026, HSS has been PMC since 2021, and collections from Pan Borneo/ECRL were already improving. Newer wins are smaller but strategically important: RM22 million PMC for a Bagan Datuk data center, RM30.5 million Jeniang Water Transfer Scheme consultancy through 2030, water and flood mitigation work, Bayan Lepas LRT packages, and an Adani Infra MOU for potential India infrastructure collaboration. None of these alone changes valuation, but together they show HSS is no longer just a stale MRT proxy.

There is also a label-change option. The 95MW Hilir Perak solar JV gives HSS a 40% stake in a 21-year PPA-backed renewable asset targeted for commercial operation in October 2027. Near term, it may depress associate earnings because of financing costs. Longer term, it can make HSS less of a pure lump-sum consulting name and more of a hybrid engineering and recurring-infrastructure-income vehicle. That deserves no heroic valuation today, but it is useful optionality.

The clean upside case is operating leverage. HSS earned RM25 million PATAMI on RM201 million revenue in FY2024 before FY2025’s impairment drag. If FY2026 to FY2027 revenue moves toward RM260 million to RM300 million as data center, water, Pan Borneo, Baghdad and early MRT3 activity convert, a 9% to 10% net margin gives RM24 million to RM30 million profit. On 508.5 million shares, that is 4.7 to 5.9 sen EPS. A 13x to 14x multiple is enough for RM0.65 to RM0.83 per share. The stock does not need blue-sky assumptions to work.

The bull case needs two confirmations: Baghdad cash arrives and MRT3 procurement momentum returns by late 2026 or 2027. Under that setup, revenue can push toward RM380 million to RM420 million by FY2028, with RM40 million to RM48 million PATAMI. That is 7.9 to 9.4 sen EPS. At 14x to 15x, HSS is a RM1.10 to RM1.40 stock, before assigning much value to solar or Adani-related optionality.

The main risks are serious. Contract assets may keep rising, forcing more revolving credit. Baghdad could become a long-dated receivable problem. MRT3 could drift again. HSS Integrated is also named in the MEX II sukuk litigation, which management says should not materially affect operations, but it is still an overhang. Book value is not a hard floor because a large part of equity is goodwill and working capital, not excess cash. Retail sentiment looks thin and erratic rather than deeply institutionalized, which helps upside reflexivity but hurts liquidity.

Still, this is a better asymmetry than the headline P/E suggests. HSS is not a balance-sheet bargain. It is an ignored backlog-conversion and cash-collection option with real contracts, a battered share price, and several 2026 to 2027 catalysts.

Current price: RM0.455.

Bear target: RM0.28, downside 38%. Baghdad cash fails to arrive, MRT3 slips further, earnings fall below RM10 million, and the market values HSS as a stressed consultancy.

Base target: RM0.75, upside 65%. FY2027 profit normalizes to RM27 million, EPS reaches about 5.3 sen, and the stock trades at 14x earnings as cash flow stabilizes.

Bull target: RM1.30, upside 186%. Baghdad becomes collectible, MRT3 moves into full procurement and execution, revenue exceeds RM380 million, PATAMI reaches RM45 million, and HSS trades near 15x forward earnings.
